tasks.chapterTitle: The Word Illuminated: Form and Function of Qurʾanic Manuscripts from the Seventh to Seventeenth Centuries (Cover to Foreword)
Introduction: Current Perspectives on Qurʾanic Manuscripts
Asṭār al-awwalīn: The Qurʾanic Handwritten Tradition and Its Beginnings
Ghaznavid Imperial Qurʾan Manuscripts: The Shaping of a Local Style
Illuminated Qurʾan Manuscripts of Late Medieval Rum (Thirteenth to Fourteenth Centuries)
A Luxury Market? Yaqut al-Mustaʿsimi’s Qurʾans
Sultan Öljeitü’s Baghdad Qurʾan: A Life History
Mamluk Qurʾans: Splendor and Opulence of the Islamic Book
A Sixteenth-Century Shiraz Masterpiece: Chester Beatty’s Ruzbihan Qurʾan and Following Chapters

messages.content:
### Summary: The Art of the Qurʾanic Manuscript

This collection of essays explores the Qurʾan not only as a sacred text but as a material object—a *muṣḥaf*—that serves as a profound symbol of piety, political authority, and religious legitimacy. The following key points outline the evolution and function of these manuscripts:

*   **Canonization and Formal Evolution:** Over centuries, Qurʾanic manuscripts underwent stylistic shifts that eventually led to a standardized template. Calligraphers played a pivotal role in this process by establishing "calligraphic lineages" (*silsilas*) that traced their techniques back to historical masters like Yaqut al-Mustaʿsimi, ensuring visual continuity across the Islamic world [S.14, S.15].
*   **The Intersection of Materiality and Craft:** The creation of luxury Qurʾans involved immense material investment, including the use of gold, parchment, and intricate bindings. Artistic choices were often deliberate; for example, Ottoman artists sometimes "updated" historic manuscripts by adding contemporary illuminations while preserving the original calligraphic text [S.6, S.14].
*   **The Role of Patrons and Artists:** Manuscript production was deeply influenced by the relationship between artists and elite patrons (sultans, viziers, and princes). Patrons often used the commissioning and endowment of lavish Qurʾans to assert their status, piety, and dynastic power. Conversely, market forces—such as the demand for high-quality works for private libraries—also drove innovation and professional collaboration among scribes [S.15, S.16].
*   **Performance and *Baraka*:** Qurʾanic manuscripts were rarely static; they were living objects meant to be recited and displayed. In settings like mosques and tombs, these volumes served as instruments of *baraka* (divine blessing). Whether carried in processions or housed in dedicated architectural shrines for continuous recitation, the manuscripts acted as a bridge between the physical codex and the oral nature of the revelation [S.6, S.17].
*   **Contextualizing Research:** Modern scholarship now emphasizes moving beyond purely stylistic analysis. By integrating codicological, paleographic, and historical research, scholars can uncover the "biographies" of individual manuscripts, revealing how these works functioned within specific social, political, and spiritual contexts throughout the seventh to seventeenth centuries [S.13, S.15].

Lesson 1 — The Word Illuminated - Presentation4 slides
The Word Illuminated
  • Examining the artistic and religious history of Qurʾanic manuscripts.
  • Tracing the production and function from the 7th to 17th centuries.

This volume, titled *The Word Illuminated: Form and Function of Qurʾanic Manuscripts from the Seventh to Seventeenth Centuries*, presents a collection of scholarly papers from a 2016 symposium held at the Smithsonian’s Arthur M. Sackler Gallery. Edited by Simon Rettig and Sana Mirza, the work explores the artistic, historical, and religious significance of luxury Qurʾanic manuscripts produced between the 7th and 17th centuries [S.2, S.6, S.9].

Key themes and objectives of the work include:

Materiality and Craftsmanship
The contributors analyze the physical creation of these manuscripts, focusing on the use of high-quality materials such as gold and parchment. They also examine the development of specialized scripts, detailed illumination, and high-level bookbinding techniques that transformed these volumes into luxury objects [S.2, S.6].

Socio-Political and Religious Significance
The essays emphasize that these manuscripts were more than liturgical texts; they functioned as symbols of piety, political authority, and legitimacy. The authors investigate how manuscripts were used as diplomatic gifts, war prizes, or royal treasures, and describe the concept of *baraka* (divine blessing), which motivated owners or patrons to endow these books to mosques and religious structures [S.2, S.6, S.9].

Historical Context and Provenance
The research covers a vast geographic range and timeline, from the early Islamic period to the Ottoman Empire. By investigating the history of individual volumes, contributors highlight how these manuscripts acquired a "layered afterlife" as they changed ownership over centuries, enriching their identity and historical value [S.2, S.6, S.9].

Academic Contribution
This publication serves as a comprehensive resource for understanding the formal and conceptual singularity of Qurʾanic codices. It incorporates diverse perspectives—including the study of specific masterworks, regional styles (such as Ghaznavid or Mamluk), and the role of artistic patronage—to contextualize the Islamic book within its broader historical environment [S.7, S.9].

This section outlines the editorial conventions used in the volume to ensure consistency and clarity. The key points are as follows:

Date Formatting
Dates are provided in both Islamic (Hijri) and Gregorian (Western) formats. Per the Islamic calendar, year 1 is equivalent to 622 CE. References to the Persian (Solar Hijri) calendar are marked with the abbreviation “Sh.”

Transliteration and Spelling
The text utilizes a modified version of the *Encyclopaedia of Islam* system for Arabic, Persian, and Ottoman Turkish. Specific adjustments include omitting subscript bars, using “q” instead of “ḳ,” and using “j” instead of “dj.”

Diacritics and Italics
Names appear without diacritics, with the sole exceptions of the ʿayn and hamza. Foreign terms already adopted into common English usage are neither italicized nor written with diacritics.

Pluralization
A limited number of Arabic loanwords retain their original plural forms.

This section provides an overview of the significance of historical Qurʾanic manuscripts, drawing from the 2016 exhibition "The Art of the Qurʾan" and its accompanying symposium. 

Key themes include:

Historical Context and Origin
The manuscripts discussed were produced between the 7th and 17th centuries across a region stretching from Afghanistan to the Mediterranean. Many of these works eventually entered the Ottoman realm, where they were endowed to religious foundations, leading to their preservation in the Museum of Turkish and Islamic Arts in Istanbul (p. 13).

Material and Cultural Significance
These manuscripts, termed *maṣāḥif*, are viewed as both works of art and objects embedded within specific social, political, and religious contexts. Studying them offers a way to explore the history of production, the biographies of patrons and artists, and the function of these texts as symbols of piety (p. 13).

Evolution of the Manuscript
While modern printed Qurʾans appear uniform, the visual history of the manuscript is characterized by gradual transformations. By the 16th century, specific calligraphic and decorative styles had crystalized (p. 14).

Case Study: The Ottoman Adaptation
A notable example of this evolution is a 1344–1345 Qurʾan by ʿAbdallah al-Sayrafi. In 1555, the Ottoman grand vizier Rüstem Pasha commissioned court artists to augment the work. While the original text remained untouched, the manuscript was remounted and adorned with new, contemporary Ottoman illustrations and marginalia. This demonstrates a balance between deep reverence for established calligraphic traditions and the application of contemporaneous aesthetic trends (p. 14).

The provided text explores the history, production, and cultural significance of Qurʾanic manuscripts (*muṣḥaf*), focusing on their evolution as both religious artifacts and instruments of political power.

Evolution of the *Muṣḥaf* and Artistic Innovation
The production of Qurʾanic manuscripts followed distinct phases, notably starting in the seventh century with early paleographic developments, followed by the eleventh-century shift to vertical formats and rounded scripts under the Ghaznavids, and concluding with formal consolidations during the Mamluk and Ottoman periods [S.15]. Key calligraphers, such as Yaqut al-Mustaʿsimi, were instrumental in canonizing styles, creating lineages (*silsilas*) that linked later artists to historical masters [S.16].

Artist-Patron Relationships
Manuscript production was deeply influenced by the goals of ruling elites. Rulers, princes, and viziers often directed the aesthetic choices of artists to affirm dynastic legitimacy, regional identity, or personal piety [S.15]. This relationship is exemplified by the Timurid era, where the scale and craftsmanship of manuscripts functioned as extensions of architectural monumentalism, and by the Ottoman period, where the production of specific suras, such as *sura al-Anʿām*, became tied to rituals surrounding the Sultan [S.15, S.16].

Manuscripts as Performative and Sacred Objects
Qurʾanic copies were not merely archival items but active participants in religious life. They were used in processions, displayed in tombs, and recited in mosques to generate *baraka* (blessing) [S.17]. The materiality of these books, including their acoustic and visual presentation, played a crucial role in creating multisensory sacred experiences, whether through public recitation or personal, intimate study [S.18].

Political Power and Legitimacy
Manuscripts held significant weight as symbols of religious and political authority. Attributing manuscripts to early figures like ʿUthman ibn ʿAffan or ʿAli ibn Abi Talib lent them immense prestige [S.19, S.20]. Political entities frequently used the gifting and endowment of luxurious Qurʾans to signal sovereign legitimacy, assert influence over the Muslim community, and, in the case of Safavid-Ottoman relations, express sectarian and cultural competition through deliberate stylistic choices like the use of *nastaʿlīq* [S.20].

This section explores the challenges of identifying original early Qur'anic manuscripts and the material evidence that scholars use to study them.

**The Loss of Historical Knowledge**
Despite legends surrounding the "muṣḥaf of ʿUthman"—the copies attributed to the third Caliph—historical evidence confirms that the physical appearance and scripts of these original manuscripts were largely lost by the twelfth century. The jurisconsult Malik b. Anas (d. 796) famously stated that the original copy had already vanished during his lifetime (p. 27). Over time, the inability to distinguish genuine early scripts led to the mislabeling of many "ancient" manuscripts as ʿUthmanic. 

**The Limitation of Historical Accounts**
Medieval historical records fail to describe the technical or material aspects of early Qur'anic production. Because of this, later scholars reduced early scripts to the generic term "Kufic" and struggled to accurately document the development of the *rasm* (the consonantal skeleton of the text), often conflating information from different chronological periods (p. 27).

**Material Evidence and Preservation**
Despite the lack of contemporary descriptions, scholarly understanding has been bolstered by the discovery of "genizah-like" storage deposits within major mosques. Legal requirements regarding the disposal of damaged sacred texts led to the preservation of fragments in places like the Umayyad Mosque in Damascus. These collections, such as the one now held by the Museum of Turkish and Islamic Arts (TIEM) in Istanbul, provide the material evidence necessary to reconstruct the history of Qur'anic transmission during the Umayyad and early Abbasid periods (p. 27).

The provided document features an article titled "Illuminated Qur'an Manuscripts of Late Medieval Rum (Thirteenth to Fourteenth Centuries)" by Cailah Jackson, which covers pages 55 through 80.

The key points of this section are as follows:

This study examines the artistic development of Qur'anic manuscript production in Anatolia during the thirteenth and fourteenth centuries, a period associated with the Seljuks of Rum and their successors.

The author emphasizes that during this era, the region experienced a distinct evolution in the aesthetic and material quality of sacred manuscripts. This transformation was influenced by local political shifts and the convergence of various artistic traditions.

A central focus is the analysis of illumination techniques, structural design, and codicology. These physical characteristics are used to trace how scholarly and artistic output functioned as an instrument of both religious piety and royal patronage in the medieval landscape.

The text highlights how manuscript scholarship serves as a lens to view the broader social and historical contexts of the Islamic world, demonstrating how these volumes were preserved, endowed, and repurposed across generations to transmit religious significance.

The section covering pages 81 to 96 focuses on the development and market presence of luxury Qurʾanic manuscripts, with a specific emphasis on the contributions of the celebrated calligrapher Yaqut al-Mustaʿsimi.

Key points include:

The professionalization of the manuscript market: A significant portion of this section examines whether a distinct "luxury market" for Qurʾanic manuscripts existed during this period, particularly through the work of high-profile scribes. 

The legacy of Yaqut al-Mustaʿsimi: This section explores how Yaqut al-Mustaʿsimi’s distinct calligraphic style influenced the production and valuation of Qurʾanic codices. His ability to refine and standardize scripts made his works highly sought after, establishing a template for luxury production that redefined aesthetic standards of the time.

Material production and value: The text discusses the interplay between the prestige of the calligrapher and the physical materiality of the books. The demand for these manuscripts was driven by the integration of skilled craftsmanship, high-quality materials, and the socio-religious status associated with owning or endowing such volumes.

Scholarly context: The analysis serves to frame the emergence of professional scribal networks as a critical transition in the history of Islamic book arts. By positioning Yaqut’s output within a market framework, the authors demonstrate how individual artistic identity became a primary driver of the value and circulation of these religious texts.

The section covering pages 97 through 122 focuses on the life history of Sultan Öljeitü’s Baghdad Qur'an, authored by Sheila S. Blair. 

Key points include:

The analysis centers on the "Baghdad Qur'an" commissioned by the Ilkhanid ruler Sultan Öljeitü. This manuscript is significant not only for its artistic execution but also for its status as a vital historical object that records the complex intersection of patronage, religious piety, and political authority.

The text tracks the manuscript's "afterlife," emphasizing that these volumes were rarely static objects. As they moved through different hands—passing from royal libraries to mosques, tombs, or as diplomatic gifts—their functions evolved. The manuscript served as an instrument of legitimacy for the patron, intended to perpetuate divine blessing (baraka) for the owner and the religious complexes where it was eventually endowed.

By documenting the physical and historical journey of this specific Qur'an, the study illustrates how manuscripts were treated as significant, layered artifacts. These items functioned as more than just religious texts; they were potent symbols of status and authority that connected the material world of the patron to the spiritual significance of the divine word.

The section titled "Mamluk Qurʾans: Splendor and Opulence of the Islamic Book" (p. 123) examines the evolution and aesthetic characteristics of Qurʾanic manuscripts produced under Mamluk rule.

Key points include:

The Mamluk period represents a pinnacle in the production of luxury manuscripts, characterized by extreme opulence. Scribes and illuminators utilized precious materials—most notably gold and high-quality paper—to create works that served as manifestations of religious devotion and political prestige.

The manuscripts often functioned as diplomatic instruments or royal endowments, intended to secure divine blessing (baraka) for their patrons. By commissioning such exquisite volumes, the Mamluk elite sought to cement their legitimacy and demonstrate their role as protectors of Islamic tradition.

The technical refinement of these books, including meticulously tooled bindings and intricate illumination, reflects the high degree of specialization present in Mamluk artistic workshops. These physical attributes were not merely decorative; they were essential components of the manuscript's identity, transforming the physical object into a powerful symbol of authority and piety.

The production of these Qur’ans was deeply embedded in the social, religious, and political landscape of the time. As these books circulated through mosques, tombs, and libraries, their "afterlife"—marked by changes in ownership and purpose—continued to reinforce their status as revered treasures throughout the centuries.

This section covers the final scholarly contributions and backmatter of the publication "The Word Illuminated." The key points are summarized below:

**Scholarly Contributions (pp. 147–212)**
*   **A Sixteenth-Century Shiraz Masterpiece:** Elaine Wright examines the Ruzbihan Qurʾan held at the Chester Beatty Library, highlighting its artistic significance as an example of Shiraz manuscript production during the sixteenth century (p. 147).
*   **Qurʾanic Soundscape:** Nina Macaraig investigates the relationship between manuscript tradition and architecture by analyzing recitation programs in Mimar Sinan’s mosques, with a specific focus on the Hadım İbrahim Paşa Mosque (p. 165).
*   **The Rise of the Enʿam:** Simon Rettig explores the emergence of the Enʿam, a manuscript genre containing specific selections of suras, which gained popularity in the Ottoman Empire during the early sixteenth century (p. 185).

**Concluding Material (pp. 213–234)**
*   **About the Contributors:** This section provides biographical information for the scholars who participated in the 2016 symposium and contributed to this volume (p. 213).
*   **Index:** A comprehensive index of persons and places mentioned throughout the text is provided to assist in cross-referencing significant historical figures and locations (p. 215).
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: According to the text, what was a common reason for historical Qurʾanic manuscripts to be endowed to mosques and tombs?
options: To serve as public archives for governmental records
To perpetuate and transmit the divine blessing (baraka) associated with the manuscript
To demonstrate the artistic superiority of the local governor
To ensure the manuscripts were kept secure from invading forces
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that manuscripts were often endowed to religious complexes to 'perpetuate and transmit their exceptional baraka (divine blessing).'

questions.question: What is the significance of the 1555 reframing of the Qurʾan by Rüstem Pasha described in the introduction?
options: It highlights how historical manuscripts were 'updated' with new aesthetics while preserving the original calligraphic authority
It shows that Ottoman elites prioritized complete copies over fragmented manuscripts
It proves that 16th-century librarians intentionally destroyed original bindings to hide the manuscript's origins
It demonstrates how Ottoman artists prioritized modifying the text over maintaining the calligraphy
questions.explanation: The text explains that while Ottoman artists added new illuminations and bindings to reflect contemporaneous trends, the elegantly copied text by al-Sayrafi was left untouched, showing an appreciation for the established visual canon.

questions.question: How did the early scribal practices mentioned by François Déroche contribute to the development of the Qurʾan's identity?
options: By emphasizing that calligraphy had no impact on the religious authority of the manuscript
By establishing specific physical and visual characteristics, such as the horizontal format and unique Kufic scripts, that distinguished the Qurʾan from other writings
By abandoning all previous scripts in favor of a universal, non-local alphabet
By standardizing the text into a vertical format that matched all other contemporary administrative documents
questions.explanation: Déroche’s analysis shows how stylistic shifts, such as the adoption of the horizontal format and Kufic scripts, were used to create a unique visual identity for the mushaf that stood apart from other written documents.

questions.question: What does Alya Karame's study of Ghaznavid manuscripts reveal about the evolution of calligraphy?
options: That scribes were forced to adopt a single regional script by imperial decree
That calligraphy regressed during the 11th century due to a lack of skilled artisans
That there was a simple, linear progression from basic to complex cursive scripts
That rather than a simple progression, there was a conscious juxtaposition of different styles to assert a unique regional identity
questions.explanation: Karame identifies that these manuscripts do not follow a 'straightforward teleological march' but rather feature an 'unprecedented juxtaposition of styles' used to assert dynastic autonomy.

questions.question: Beyond their role as works of art, how are Qurʾanic manuscripts characterized in their 'afterlife'?
options: As static objects that lost their social function once they left the royal library
As primarily economic assets used to pay off diplomatic debts between empires
As symbols of piety and political authority that were part of complex social, religious, and historical contexts
As items whose value was solely determined by the cost of the parchment used
questions.explanation: The text emphasizes that as these manuscripts changed ownership, they served as 'potent symbols of piety and political and religious authority' and 'instruments of legitimacy' within their social and religious contexts.

cards.front: What is the primary objective of 'The Word Illuminated'?
cards.back: To explore the form, function, and artistic, historic, and religious contexts of Qur'anic manuscripts produced from the seventh to seventeenth centuries.

cards.front: Define 'baraka' in the context of Qur'anic manuscripts.
cards.back: Divine blessing; many Qur'anic volumes were endowed to religious complexes to perpetuate and transmit this blessing.

cards.front: What event served as the catalyst for the research presented in this volume?
cards.back: The 2016 symposium 'The Word Illuminated: Form and Function of Qur'anic Manuscripts' held at the Smithsonian's Arthur M. Sackler Gallery.

cards.front: Name two major factors that shaped the material luxury of Qur'anic manuscripts.
cards.back: The lavish use of costly materials (e.g., gold and parchment), the development of specialized scripts, intricate illumination, and meticulously tooled bindings.
The Arabic term for a codex or volume of a Qur'anic manuscript.

cards.front: Who was one of the most pivotal figures in the history of Qur'anic calligraphic schools in Egypt, Turkey, and Iran?
How were older Qur'anic manuscripts often repurposed in the Ottoman era?
cards.back: They were sometimes refurbished with new margins, chapter headings, and illuminations (e.g., the work of Karamemi) while preserving the original calligraphic text.

cards.front: What does François Déroche investigate regarding early Qur'anic history?
cards.back: The tradition of copying the Qur'an using evidence from 'genizah-like' collections (bibliothecae coranicae) in cities like Damascus, Fustat, Kairouan, and Sana'a.

cards.front: What did Gaznavid imperial Qur'ans represent during the eleventh century?
cards.back: They embodied the dynasty's assertions of autonomy, strength, and an affirmation of a regional 'Khurasani' identity.

cards.front: What are the core chronological parameters of the manuscripts studied in the volume?
cards.back: From the seventh century to the seventeenth century.

slides.title: The Word Illuminated
bullets: Examining the artistic and religious history of Qurʾanic manuscripts.
Tracing the production and function from the 7th to 17th centuries.
slides.notes: This lesson explores how Qurʾanic manuscripts served as both sacred objects and symbols of political authority.

slides.title: Introduction: Perspectives on Manuscripts
bullets: Qurʾans served as potent symbols of piety and religious authority.
Manuscripts often functioned as diplomatic gifts or endowed religious treasures.
The volume investigates the intersection of materiality, context, and patronage.
Studies highlight the complex lifecycles and 'afterlives' of these volumes (p. 13).
slides.notes: We begin by situating manuscripts within their social context, noting that they often functioned as endowed foundations that transmitted divine blessing.

slides.title: Canonization and Visual Tradition
bullets: Ottoman updates to historic volumes preserved the original text (p. 14).
Artists often maintained visual continuity with traditional calligraphic canons.
Calligraphic lineages back to Yaqut al-Mustaʿsimi solidified the Qurʾanic form.
Standardization evolved over centuries toward modern homogeneity (p. 14).
slides.notes: Even when renovating older manuscripts, artists were bound by an established visual code, reinforcing the longevity of the Qurʾanic tradition.

slides.title: Tracing the Handwritten Tradition
bullets: Early 'Uthmanic' manuscripts were gradually lost to history.
Genizah-like storage in mosques preserved crucial early fragments (p. 15).
Paleographic analysis reveals shifts in scripts and book culture.
Material evidence helps reconstruct early Umayyad and Abbasid traditions (p. 15).
slides.notes: By examining early deposits in major mosques, scholars can reconstruct the material history of the nascent Muslim empire's book production.
